title: Summer Heat Safety Tips for Portland Pet Owners
description: Portland summers can bring dangerous heat waves. Learn how to keep your dog or cat safe during hot weather from our NW Portland veterinary team.
content:
Portland's summers have become increasingly unpredictable, with heat waves that can push temperatures well above 90°F — and occasionally past 100°F. While our pets love spending time outdoors, heat-related illness can develop quickly and become life-threatening. Here's how to keep your pet safe when temperatures rise.

## Know the Risk Factors

Some pets are more vulnerable to heat than others. High-risk groups include:

- **Brachycephalic breeds** (bulldogs, pugs, Persian cats, Boston terriers) — their shortened airways make it harder to cool down through panting
- **Senior pets** and very young puppies or kittens
- **Overweight pets**
- **Pets with heart or respiratory conditions**
- **Dark-coated or thick-coated breeds**

If your pet falls into any of these categories, take extra precautions during warm weather.

## Never Leave Your Pet in a Parked Car

Even on a 75°F day, the inside of a car can reach 100°F within 10 minutes and 120°F within 30 minutes — even with windows cracked. On a 90°F day, interior temperatures can exceed 160°F. It doesn't take long for heatstroke to set in. If you see a pet in distress inside a parked car in Oregon, call 911 — Oregon law allows first responders to break a window to rescue the animal.

## Adjust Your Walk Schedule

During heat waves, walk your dog in the early morning or after sunset when temperatures are cooler. Test the pavement with your hand — if it's too hot to hold your palm on the surface for 5 seconds, it's too hot for paw pads. Asphalt can reach 150°F when the air temperature is only 85°F. Stick to grass or shaded trails whenever possible.

## Hydration and Shade

Always provide fresh, cool water — indoors and outdoors. Add ice cubes to the water bowl on especially hot days. If your pet spends time outside, make sure they have access to shade at all times. A kiddie pool in a shaded area can help dogs cool down quickly.

## Recognize the Signs of Heatstroke

Heatstroke is a veterinary emergency. Watch for:

- Excessive or heavy panting
- Drooling or thick saliva
- Bright red or pale gums
- Vomiting or diarrhea
- Staggering or lack of coordination
- Collapse or unresponsiveness

If you suspect heatstroke, move your pet to a cool area immediately, apply room-temperature water (not ice water, which can constrict blood vessels and trap heat) to the paws, belly, and ears, and head to the nearest veterinary clinic right away. Do not wait to see if they improve on their own.

## Cats and Heat

Cats are generally better at finding cool spots, but they're not immune to heat-related illness. Ensure indoor cats have access to cool rooms, and watch outdoor cats for signs of lethargy or excessive grooming (cats lick their fur to cool down, similar to sweating). If your cat is panting, that's a serious warning sign — cats rarely pant under normal circumstances.

## Summer Fun, Safely

With some planning, you and your pet can enjoy Portland's beautiful summers safely. Stick to shady parks and trails, carry water on every walk, and know your pet's limits. If the forecast calls for extreme heat, keep outdoor time short and save the long hikes for cooler days.

If your pet shows any signs of heat-related illness, call Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic at (503) 291-1757 during business hours, or visit our emergency page for after-hours resources.
